title: Lemon sugar hand scrub
subtitle: An anhydrous sugar scrub for hands with lemon essential oil and sweet almond oil
excerpt: An anhydrous sugar scrub for the hands made with granulated sugar, sweet almond oil, and a small amount of lemon essential oil. The sugar dissolves on contact with water, leaving a thin oil film. Sits next to the kitchen sink; useful for cook's hands after grating ginger or peeling garlic.
sourceNotes: Standard anhydrous sugar scrub ratio: 60-70% sugar, 25-35% carrier oil, 1-3% essential oil. Granulated sugar (not caster) gives a useful grit without being abrasive. Lemon EO at 1% is well below the IFRA dermal limit (2%) for rinse-off products.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly. Two 250 ml jars - one by the kitchen sink, one by the bathroom.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Anhydrous and shelf-stable. The lemon top note fades over months; remake every 3-4 months for the best scent.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 6 months at room temperature. The sugar separates from the oil over weeks; stir before each use. Lemon essential oil is phototoxic at high doses; the 1% in this rinse-off product is below the threshold but rinse hands well before sun exposure. Discard if the jar smells rancid or develops mould (shouldn't happen if kept dry).

glossaryTerms.term: Essential oil load for rinse-off products
glossaryTerms.definition: Rinse-off products (scrubs, soaps, body washes) tolerate higher essential oil concentrations than leave-on products because the EO contacts skin briefly and washes away. IFRA dermal limits are typically 2-4 times higher for rinse-off. A 1% lemon EO sugar scrub is comfortable; the same 1% in a leave-on balm would risk sensitisation over repeated use.

Some citrus essential oils (lemon, bergamot, lime cold-pressed) contain furocoumarins that react with UV light and cause skin sensitivity or burns. Sweet orange and steam-distilled lemon are not phototoxic; cold-pressed lemon is mildly phototoxic above 2% leave-on. A 1% rinse-off scrub is well below the threshold but still rinse hands thoroughly before sustained sun exposure.

content.text: Hand scrub belongs next to the kitchen sink, not the bathroom. The use is cook's hands after grating ginger or peeling raw garlic, gardener's hands after weeding, painter's hands after pulling tape. A teaspoon scooped onto wet palms, scrubbed for 20 seconds, rinsed. The sugar dissolves; the oil stays on the skin.
is 1% lemon - a clean kitchen-appropriate note without sensitising at this rinse-off dose. Lemon essential oil is mildly
at higher concentrations; the 1% in this scrub is below the threshold but rinse well before going out in the sun.

items.prepNote: Granulated white sugar. Caster is too fine - dissolves too fast in the jar and feels gritty rather than scrubby on the skin.
Light carrier oil. Olive oil works but has a stronger residual smell; sunflower works and is cheaper.
Stabilises the blend. Slow to oxidise; extends shelf life.
1% of total batch. Cold-pressed lemon. Skip for an unscented scrub.

content.text: Weigh the granulated sugar into the mixing bowl. In a small measuring jug, weigh the sweet almond oil, jojoba oil, and lemon essential oil. Stir the oils together. Pour the oil blend over the sugar. Stir with a silicone spatula until the sugar is uniformly coated and the mixture looks like wet sand. Spoon into a clean dry 250 ml jam jar. Lid and label.

Wet hands. Scoop a heaped teaspoon of scrub into the palm. Rub gently across the back of the hands and between the fingers for 20-30 seconds. Rinse with warm water; the sugar dissolves cleanly. Pat dry. The oil film keeps hands soft through the next wash. Stir the jar with a clean dry spoon if the oil pools above the sugar between uses.

Sealed jar at room temperature, away from sunlight. 6 months. Keep the spoon dry; water in the jar can grow mould given enough time. The lemon top note fades after 3-4 months; the scrub remains useable but smells of plain almond oil at 6 months.
